replacementToys.name: Chewy Necklaces & Pendants
replacementToys.description: Safe, non-toxic silicone necklaces that provide oral sensory input for children who bite
benefits: Reduces biting of self and others

replacementToys.name: Chewy Tubes
replacementToys.description: Textured tubes designed for safe chewing and biting, great for teething and oral motor development
benefits: Multiple textures for different sensory needs
Promotes jaw strength and oral motor skills

replacementToys.name: Fidget Spinners & Poppers
replacementToys.description: Quiet tactile toys that keep hands busy and provide sensory feedback

replacementToys.name: Weighted Lap Pad
replacementToys.description: Small weighted blanket for lap that provides calming deep pressure input

replacementToys.name: Stress Balls & Squeeze Toys
replacementToys.description: Squishy balls and toys that provide resistance and tactile feedback

replacementToys.name: Sensory Bins (Rice, Beans, Water Beads)
replacementToys.description: Containers filled with materials for tactile exploration and play
benefits: Can hide small toys for added motivation

replacementToys.name: Noise-Canceling Headphones
replacementToys.description: Reduces overwhelming sounds for children with auditory sensitivities

replacementToys.name: Compression Vest or Shirt
replacementToys.description: Tight-fitting garment that provides constant deep pressure input

preferredActivities.name: Bubble Play
preferredActivities.description: Simple, engaging activity that teaches requesting, turn-taking, and following directions
teachingTips: Hold bubbles out of reach to encourage requesting (words, signs, or PECS)
Model blowing and have child imitate your actions
Practice 'my turn, your turn' for turn-taking
Use 'ready, set, go!' to teach waiting and anticipation
Follow child's gaze and blow bubbles where they look
preferredActivities.whyItWorks: Bubbles are naturally motivating for most children and create lots of opportunities for communication and social interaction.

preferredActivities.name: Shape Sorters
preferredActivities.description: Classic toy that teaches matching, fine motor skills, and problem-solving
teachingTips: Start with hand-over-hand prompting for success
Use 'in' as a simple verbal target
Let child request shapes ('want circle')
Celebrate success enthusiastically
Gradually fade prompts as child learns
preferredActivities.whyItWorks: Provides clear cause and effect, immediate success, and can be adapted to many skill levels.

preferredActivities.name: Sensory Play (Play-Doh, Kinetic Sand)
preferredActivities.description: Open-ended tactile play that calms and engages
materials: Cookie cutters, rolling pin, plastic tools
teachingTips: Control access to materials to encourage requesting
Model actions and have child imitate (roll, smash, cut)
Name actions and objects during play
Allow free exploration - follow child's lead
Use as a calming activity when child is overstimulated
preferredActivities.whyItWorks: Naturally calming and engaging, provides rich sensory input, and creates natural teaching moments.

preferredActivities.name: Simple Puzzles (3-12 pieces)
preferredActivities.description: Structured activity that builds problem-solving and persistence
teachingTips: Start with puzzles that are slightly easy to build confidence
Use hand-over-hand to prevent frustration initially
Teach 'help' as a functional communication skill
Celebrate completion with enthusiasm
Gradually increase difficulty as skills improve
preferredActivities.whyItWorks: Clear beginning and end, builds persistence, and success is immediately visible.

preferredActivities.name: Cause & Effect Toys
preferredActivities.description: Toys that respond immediately to child's actions (pop-up toys, musical toys)
teachingTips: Model the action first, then prompt imitation
Pause and wait for child to request 'more' or 'again'
Use anticipation ('ready, set, GO!')
Let child activate the toy themselves for motivation
Exaggerate your excitement to increase engagement
preferredActivities.whyItWorks: Immediate feedback is highly reinforcing, teaches children that their actions have effects on the environment.

preferredActivities.name: Pretend Play (Kitchen, Doctor, etc.)
preferredActivities.description: Imaginative play that builds social skills and language
teachingTips: Start with functional play (real actions) before symbolic
Model pretend actions and narrate what you're doing
Join child's play and follow their lead
Gradually introduce new pretend concepts
Practice scripts ('Hello! How are you?')
preferredActivities.whyItWorks: Develops important social and language skills in a fun context, builds imagination and flexibility.

preferredActivities.name: Interactive Books & Reading
preferredActivities.description: Shared reading that builds language and bonding
materials: Books with flaps, textures, or sounds
teachingTips: Let child turn pages to increase engagement
Point to pictures and label them
Ask simple questions ('Where's the dog?')
Use animated voices and sounds
Read the same book repeatedly (children love repetition)
Follow child's interest - it's okay to skip pages
preferredActivities.whyItWorks: Builds language in a low-pressure, cozy context. Repetition helps learning.

preferredActivities.name: Gross Motor Play (Jumping, Running, Dancing)
preferredActivities.description: Active play that provides sensory input and teaches body control
teachingTips: Model movements and have child imitate
Give simple directions ('jump!', 'stop!', 'go!')
Use music to make it more motivating
Build in movement breaks throughout the day
Allow rough play (crashing into pillows) for proprioceptive input
preferredActivities.whyItWorks: Provides organizing sensory input, burns energy, and teaches body control. Great for regulating behavior.
